name: Metatron
shortDescription: Metatron is a prominent angel in Jewish mysticism and some branches of Christianity, often described as the highest of the angels and the celestial scribe. He is believed to be the transformed Enoch from the Hebrew Bible.
description: Metatron is a powerful and complex figure in Jewish mystical traditions, particularly in Kabbalah and related texts. He is often described as the highest-ranking angel in the heavenly hierarchy, situated close to God. A prevailing tradition identifies Metatron with the biblical figure Enoch, who was taken by God and transformed into an angel. This transformation is detailed in texts like 3 Enoch (also known as the Hebrew Book of Enoch). Metatron's roles are numerous and significant. He acts as the celestial scribe, recording the deeds of humanity. He is also seen as a mediator between God and humanity, and as a guide to those who ascend to the heavens. He is associated with the Shekhinah (divine presence) and is sometimes considered a lesser YHWH. The concept of Metatron developed over centuries, drawing on biblical interpretations, apocryphal literature, and mystical traditions. He is a figure of immense authority and power, yet his origins as a human being transformed into an angel make him a relatable and accessible figure within the often-abstract realms of Jewish mysticism. He is associated with the Tree of Life and is a key figure in the Merkabah tradition. While not explicitly mentioned in the Hebrew Bible under the name Metatron, his roots are traced back to the story of Enoch in Genesis and his presence is felt throughout much of the later mystical literature. He is considered a prince of the divine presence and is seen as a guardian of the celestial realm. He is also said to wear a robe with 72 names of God embroidered on it.

sources.title: 3 Enoch, or the Hebrew Book of Enoch
sources.author: Hugo Odeberg

worshipPractices.name: Meditation and Contemplation
worshipPractices.description: While not directly worshipped, practitioners of Kabbalah and Jewish mysticism may meditate on Metatron's attributes and role to gain insight into divine wisdom and the structure of the cosmos. This practice seeks to connect with the higher spiritual realms and understand Metatron's function as an intermediary.

worshipPractices.name: Study of Sacred Texts
worshipPractices.description: Devotion to Metatron is often expressed through the study of texts that describe his nature and role, such as the Sefer Hekhalot (Books of Enoch). Interpreting these texts is considered a way to understand the divine plan and Metatron's relationship to God and humanity.

festivals.name: Rosh Hashanah
festivals.description: Some Kabbalistic traditions associate Metatron with judgment and recording of deeds, thus connecting him to Rosh Hashanah, a time of divine judgment and repentance. While not directly celebrated in Metatron's name, his presence is felt in the themes of the holiday.

cultural.modernLegacy: Metatron's image appears frequently in modern art and literature, often symbolizing esoteric knowledge and the bridge between the human and divine realms. His figure inspires contemporary interpretations of angelic hierarchies and the search for spiritual understanding in various new age movements and fictional works. He is often associated with sacred geometry and healing.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Both figures serve as intermediaries between the divine and human realms, and are associated with wisdom, knowledge, and sacred writings. Hermes Trismegistus, like Metatron, is a figure often connected to esoteric and mystical traditions.

Thoth is the Egyptian god of wisdom, writing, and knowledge, serving as a scribe and record-keeper for the gods. This parallels Metatron's role as a heavenly scribe and keeper of divine records, particularly the Book of Life.

Sraosha is a Yazata (angelic being) in Zoroastrianism who embodies obedience, righteousness, and is a messenger of Ahura Mazda. Similar to Metatron, Sraosha acts as an intermediary between the divine and humanity, guiding souls and upholding divine law.

Chitragupta is a deity in Hinduism who is the record keeper of Yama, the god of death, keeping track of the good and bad deeds of humans. This is similar to Metatron's role as a heavenly scribe who records the deeds of humanity.

associations.significance: Represents the Sephirot of the Tree of Life, which Metatron is said to oversee as a divine architect.

associations.name: Sun
associations.significance: Symbolizes Metatron's radiant light and the divine energy he channels from the Godhead.

associations.name: White
associations.significance: Represents purity, wisdom, and Metatron's role as a mediator between God and humanity.

associations.name: Transformation
associations.significance: Reflects Metatron's origin as Enoch, a human transformed into an angelic being.

associations.name: Fire
associations.significance: Symbolizes divine judgment, purification, and the intense spiritual energy associated with Metatron.

associations.name: Phoenix
associations.significance: Represents rebirth and spiritual ascension, echoing Metatron's transformation from Enoch.

associations.name: The Voice of God
associations.significance: He is considered the direct communicator and scribe of God.

companions.name: Enoch
companions.description: Enoch is Metatron's human form, as Metatron is believed to be the transformed Enoch who ascended to heaven and became an angel of high rank. He protects Enoch's legacy and existence.

companions.name: Sandalphon
companions.description: Sandalphon is often considered a twin brother or close companion to Metatron. Both angels are said to have been human prophets who ascended to angelic status, working together in the celestial realm.

companions.name: Shekinah
companions.description: The Shekinah, the divine presence, is closely associated with Metatron, as he is seen as a mediator between God and creation. Metatron helps to manifest the Shekinah's presence in the world.

companions.name: Angelic Hosts
companions.description: As the highest-ranking angel, Metatron commands legions of angels who carry out his will and the decrees of God. They serve as messengers and enforcers of divine law.

companions.name: The Scribe's Quill
companions.description: Metatron is often associated with the role of a divine scribe, recording the deeds of humanity and the decrees of God. The quill assists in this function.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Transformation: Enoch to Metatron
extendedContent.content:
The most prevalent origin story of Metatron traces back to the biblical figure of Enoch, the seventh patriarch after Adam. Genesis 5:24 states that "Enoch walked with God; and he was not, for God took him." This cryptic verse became the foundation for elaborations in later Jewish literature, particularly in the Apocrypha and Pseudepigrapha, and culminating in the Kabbalistic tradition. The texts, especially 3 Enoch (also known as the Hebrew Book of Enoch), elaborate on Enoch's ascent to heaven and subsequent transformation into the archangel Metatron. This transformation is not merely a change in form but a profound elevation in status and power. Enoch, a righteous human being, is deemed worthy to stand before God and receives a divine name, becoming a celestial being second only to God himself.

The specifics of this transformation involve Enoch being clothed in garments of light, granted immense knowledge and wisdom, and appointed as a scribe of righteousness. He is given dominion over all the angels and serves as a mediator between God and humanity. 3 Enoch depicts a detailed account of Enoch's heavenly journey, revealing the secrets of the cosmos and the divine will. The transformation narrative emphasizes the potential for human beings to attain spiritual heights through piety and devotion, though it is crucial to note that Metatron's unique status remains an exception rather than a general expectation.

The significance of this origin story lies in its bridging of the human and divine realms. Metatron, as a transformed human, represents a tangible connection between the earthly and the celestial. He becomes an accessible figure for those seeking divine guidance and intercession. However, the identification of Metatron with Enoch has also sparked debate and controversy within Jewish thought, with some scholars questioning its theological implications and potential for blurring the lines between creator and creation.

extendedContent.title: Role in Cosmic Order and Divine Scribe
extendedContent.content:
Metatron's role within the cosmic order is multifaceted and profoundly significant. He is often depicted as the 'Prince of the Presence,' standing closest to God and acting as his chief minister. This position grants him unparalleled access to the divine realm and allows him to oversee the functioning of the universe. He is considered the architect of creation, responsible for executing God's plans and ensuring the harmonious operation of all things. His presence is perceived as essential for maintaining the balance between the divine and the material worlds.

One of Metatron's key functions is that of a divine scribe. He is entrusted with recording the deeds of humanity, both good and bad, and presenting them before God for judgment. This role is reminiscent of the ancient scribes who meticulously documented important events and maintained records in royal courts. Metatron's role as a divine scribe underscores the importance of ethical conduct and the accountability of individuals for their actions. He is also a guardian of sacred knowledge, holding the keys to the mysteries of the universe and revealing them only to those who are worthy.

Furthermore, Metatron is often seen as a mediator between God and humanity. He carries the prayers of the faithful to the divine throne and transmits divine blessings and guidance to those who seek it. This mediatory role makes him a powerful figure for those seeking solace, protection, and spiritual enlightenment. However, it's crucial to differentiate this role from the concept of divine worship. While Metatron is revered and respected, he is not to be worshipped as a deity. He remains a creation of God, albeit a highly exalted one, and his role is to serve as a conduit for divine grace and wisdom.

extendedContent.title: Iconography and Symbolic Representation
extendedContent.content:
Unlike deities in other polytheistic religions, Metatron does not have a codified iconography or a universally accepted visual representation. The absence of consistent visual depictions stems from the inherent limitations in portraying a figure of such immense power and transcendence. Instead of physical attributes, Metatron is often represented through symbolic imagery and esoteric descriptions that convey his unique qualities and functions. These representations are primarily found within Kabbalistic texts and mystical traditions.

Common symbolic representations include the 'Merkabah' or divine chariot, which signifies his association with the celestial realm and his role as a guide to the heavens. He is also linked to the 'Tree of Life,' a central symbol in Kabbalah that represents the structure of the universe and the interconnectedness of all things. Metatron is sometimes associated with the highest sephirah, Keter (Crown), symbolizing his proximity to the divine source. Numerical symbolism also plays a significant role. The numerical value of Metatron's name in Hebrew gematria is equal to that of Shaddai, one of the names of God, reinforcing his close relationship with the divine.

Light and fire are frequently used to describe Metatron's appearance. He is often depicted as being clothed in garments of pure light, radiating with divine energy. This imagery emphasizes his spiritual purity and his role as a beacon of illumination. His fiery nature symbolizes his power and his ability to transform and purify. While specific artistic renditions of Metatron are rare, these symbolic representations provide a framework for understanding his multifaceted nature and his profound significance within Jewish mystical thought.

extendedContent.title: Metatron in Kabbalah and Mystical Traditions
extendedContent.content:
Metatron holds a prominent position within Kabbalah, the Jewish mystical tradition. He is considered a key figure in understanding the structure of the universe and the relationship between God and creation. Kabbalists view Metatron as an intermediary between the Ein Sof (the infinite, unknowable God) and the sephirot, the ten emanations of God's attributes through which the divine manifests in the world. He is seen as the architect and administrator of these divine energies, ensuring their proper flow and balance.

In Kabbalistic cosmology, Metatron is often associated with the Sefirah of Keter (Crown), the highest of the sephirot, representing the divine will and the source of all creation. He acts as a bridge between the unmanifested God and the lower sephirot, facilitating the flow of divine energy into the world. He is also connected to the Sefirah of Yesod (Foundation), serving as a channel for the energies flowing down from the higher sephirot to Malkuth (Kingdom), the sefirah representing the physical world.

Moreover, Metatron plays a crucial role in Kabbalistic practices such as meditation and ascent to the divine. Practitioners may seek Metatron's guidance and protection during their spiritual journeys, invoking his name and visualizing his presence to aid them in traversing the celestial realms. He is seen as a powerful ally in overcoming obstacles and attaining spiritual enlightenment. However, Kabbalistic teachings emphasize the importance of approaching Metatron with reverence and humility, recognizing his status as a created being and avoiding any form of idol worship.
